paper_title: Sediment Sampling and Diatom Analysis in Lake Shira and Lake Utichye-3

methods.description: Process for collecting sediment samples from Lake Shira and Lake Utichye-3 using a box corer, followed by storage and preparation for analysis.

steps.description: Sediment samples were collected in May 2011 (Lake Shira) and July 2012 (Lake Utichye-3) using a box corer designed at the Institute for Biology of Inland Waters RAS (Borok).
steps.searchable_summary: collect sediment samples using a box corer

steps.description: In the laboratory, plastic tubes were used to take cores from the samples. Both ends of each tube were sealed, and the tubes were stored at a temperature of +4°C in a strictly vertical position.
steps.searchable_summary: store sediment cores in plastic tubes at +4°C

steps.description: The cores were cut longitudinally and divided into halves with two thin stainless steel plates inserted into the cleft. After the core was separated, the plates were removed by moving them transversely.
steps.searchable_summary: cut and split sediment cores longitudinally

steps.description: The core halves were kept in air under weak light for 24 h to achieve the most distinct colour differences. Then, a colour photograph of each core with a fixed millimetre ruler was taken.
steps.searchable_summary: photograph sediment cores with millimetre ruler

steps.description: The core halves were then sliced into 5-10 mm thick transverse samples. All samples were stored in the dark at a temperature of -20°C in vacuum-sealed polyethylene bags.
steps.searchable_summary: slice and store sediment samples at -20°C

methods.description: Procedure for analyzing the species composition and abundance of diatoms in the bottom sediments using microscopy and identification guides.

steps.description: Three 1.5 ml samples of sediment were taken from each trap. The samples were placed in centrifuge tubes and dried in a drying cabinet for 24 h at a temperature of 100°C.
steps.searchable_summary: dry sediment samples in a drying cabinet

steps.description: The remaining sediment was weighed to determine its dry weight, and an aliquot of approximately 0.05 mg was taken. It was treated with a 30% hydrogen peroxide solution while being heated in a solid state thermostat to a temperature of 90°C for 4 h, with hydrogen peroxide added continually.
steps.searchable_summary: treat sediment with hydrogen peroxide at 90°C

steps.description: After cooling the samples, the hydrogen peroxide was removed by washing the samples 5 times in distilled water using centrifugation; then, each sample was diluted with distilled water to achieve a volume of 1.5 ml.
steps.searchable_summary: wash and dilute sediment samples with distilled water

steps.description: The valves of the diatoms were counted and photographed using a light microscope Axiovert (Zeiss, Germany) equipped with an oil immersion objective (×100/n.a.1.4,DIC) and an electron microscope TM3000 (Hitachi, Japan).
steps.searchable_summary: count and photograph diatom valves using microscopes

steps.description: Determination of the sedimentation rate was based on the total number of cells in the trap, the time of deployment of the trap, and the cross-sectional area of the trap. These data were compared with the available data on the diatom sedimentation rate in 2012.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate sedimentation rate from cell count and trap data

steps.description: Diatoms were identified using identification guides and taxonomic keys. The correctness of the authors' species names and the currently used species names were checked at http://algaebase.org.
steps.searchable_summary: identify diatom species using taxonomic keys and verify names online
